title: Semantic multi-page site

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Semantic multi-page site; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Semantic multi-page site
sources.claim: A small site needs consistent document structure, navigation, titles, landmarks, valid links, accessible media and forms, and a repeatable review checklist.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Semantic multi-page site
sources.claim: A small site needs consistent document structure, navigation, titles, landmarks, valid links, accessible media and forms, and a repeatable review checklist.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A small site needs consistent document structure, navigation, titles, landmarks, valid links, accessible media and forms, and a repeatable review checklist.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A portfolio site tests every navigation path from its deployment subdirectory and audits keyboard flow on each template. Your goal is to deliver and explain a validated, keyboard-reviewed multi-page HTML site with documented non-goals. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Metadata and accessibility audit; Images and media; Data tables; Forms and native controls; Progressive baseline; Audio, video, tracks and embeds; Captions and context; Native constraints and error semantics. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A small site needs consistent document structure, navigation, titles, landmarks, valid links, accessible media and forms, and a repeatable review checklist. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Page-by-page success can hide cross-page inconsistency, broken deployment paths, duplicate ids, stale content, or missing error states. This draft therefore rejects: If each page validates alone, the complete site's navigation and user journeys are automatically correct.

includes: Integrate document structure, navigation, media, data and forms into one coherent artifact.
Deliver and explain a validated, keyboard-reviewed multi-page HTML site with documented non-goals.
excludes: Page-by-page success can hide cross-page inconsistency, broken deployment paths, duplicate ids, stale content, or missing error states.

outcomes: Deliver and explain a validated, keyboard-reviewed multi-page HTML site with documented non-goals.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Verify the complete deployed site, including cross-page navigation, paths, consistency, content, and task flows.
